column1: # OWASP ZAP baseline rule overrides — clinipharma.com.br
# Format: <plugin_id>\t<action>\t<comment>
#   action ∈ { IGNORE, WARN, FAIL }
# IGNORE means the rule is silenced everywhere. Prefer WARN over IGNORE
# whenever we want the finding visible (in the HTML report) but not
# blocking the build.
# Every IGNORE here MUST justify why we accept the finding — drift in
# the rationale is a code-review smell. Re-evaluate annually.
# Reference: https://www.zaproxy.org/docs/alerts/
# The `__Host-csrf` cookie is INTENTIONALLY non-HttpOnly. Double-submit
# CSRF defence requires the client JS to read the cookie and echo it
# back via the X-CSRF-Token header — HttpOnly would defeat that. The
# cookie carries no auth material; only an opaque 32-byte token whose
# only purpose is to be matched against a request header. See
# `lib/security/csrf.ts:236` and `docs/security/csp.md`.

column3: (__Host-csrf must be JS-readable for double-submit CSRF; carries no auth material)

column1: # ─── Cross-Domain Misconfiguration (10098) ───────────────────────────
# Vercel sets `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on `/_next/static/**`
# (CSS chunks, JS bundles, font files, images). Those assets are
# already public to anyone who loads the site — CORS `*` only allows
# programmatic fetch from other origins of files that browsers serve
# unrestricted to anyone. There is no exposed credential or secret.
# This rule cannot be scoped to a path glob in the baseline action,
# so we suppress globally; if we ever set CORS `*` on an API endpoint
# carrying credentials, the regression will surface via CodeQL +
# integration tests, not this rule.

column3: (Static asset CORS `*` is intentional Vercel default; bundles carry no secrets)

column1: # ZAP flags two patterns in our CSP:
#   1. `style-src-attr 'unsafe-inline'` — required so React's inline
#      `style={{...}}` JSX prop renders. Without it every dynamic style
#      breaks. Cannot be hashed at build time. Documented in
#      `lib/security/csp.ts:55`.
#   2. `script-src https: http:` fallback for legacy browsers without
#      `'strict-dynamic'` support. Modern browsers ignore the fallback.
#      The `'strict-dynamic'` directive is the actual gate.
# Both are documented decisions, not regressions.

column3: (style-src-attr 'unsafe-inline' = React style prop; https: fallback = pre-strict-dynamic browsers)

column1: # We protect mutating endpoints with the Origin/Referer + double-submit
# cookie pattern (lib/security/csrf.ts), not synchroniser tokens. ZAP's
# heuristic does not recognise this pattern, so the alert fires on
# every form. Documented in docs/security/dynamic-scanning.md.
# ─── Storable but non-cacheable content (10049) ──────────────────────
# Next.js streams server components without `Cache-Control: no-store`
# on intentionally public pages (/, /login, /trust). The framework's
# default is correct for our threat model — sensitive responses already
# carry `Cache-Control: private, no-store` via dedicated handlers.

column3: (Streaming RSC default; sensitive routes set their own no-store)

column1: # ─── Cookie without SameSite attribute (10054) ───────────────────────
# Supabase SSR sets short-lived auth cookies WITHOUT SameSite at the
# JWT-cookie layer (the host-level SameSite=Lax default applies). The
# CSRF mitigation lives one layer higher (Origin + double-submit), so
# this finding is not a security gap for our model. Tracked: revisit
# when @supabase/ssr exposes a SameSite=Strict opt-in.

column3: (Supabase SSR cookies — SameSite via browser default; CSRF via Origin+double-submit)

column1: # Already enforced via next.config.ts securityHeaders. ZAP sometimes
# misses it on streaming responses where the header arrives in the
# initial chunk. Keep as WARN so a real regression still surfaces.

column3: (Header set in next.config.ts; streaming may delay first-chunk inspection)
